content:
₹100 Crore Order. ₹0 Revenue.
A company announces that it has won a ₹100 crore EPC contract.
The stock jumps 6%.

Media headlines celebrate a "record order win."

Yet the next quarter, the company reports ₹0 revenue from that contract.

Did management overstate the announcement?

No.

Under Ind AS 115, winning a contract doesn't automatically create revenue.
Revenue is recognised only when the company transfers control of the promised goods or services to the customer.

Suppose the project will take 24 months to complete.

On the day the contract is signed:

• Order Book increases by ₹100 crore. 
• Revenue remains ₹0. 
• Profit remains ₹0.

Only as construction progresses and the performance obligation is satisfied does revenue begin appearing in the income statement.

This distinction is why experienced analysts don't confuse an order book with reported revenue.

An order book tells you what the company expects to earn.

Revenue tells you what the company has already earned.

The next time you see a company announce a large order, don't ask,
"How big is the contract?"

Ask,

"When will it actually become revenue?"
That single question often separates market excitement from financial reality.
